item.title: Oracle EBS CVE-2026-46817 Attacks, ARToken Phishing, and Japan Breach Disclosures
item.content:encoded:
Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks — Must Read

Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817, a critical unauthenticated takeover flaw in Oracle E-Business Suite, while Shadowserver reports about 950 EBS instances exposed online. Oracle patched the bug in its May 2026 security update and customers are urged to apply fixes immediately.

  • Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances have been found exposed online amid ongoing attacks exploiting CVE-2026-46817.
  • CVE-2026-46817 affects the File Transmission component of Oracle Payments in Oracle E-Business Suite and allows unauthenticated attackers with HTTP network access to take over vulnerable systems through low-complexity attacks.
  • Oracle patched CVE-2026-46817 in its May 2026 Critical Security Patch Update and urged customers to patch their systems immediately.
  • Defused reported that it observed an actor exploiting CVE-2026-46817 on Oracle E-Business honeypots over the weekend, while noting that no public proof-of-concept code is known.
  • Shadowserver said it tracks around 950 Oracle E-Business Suite instances exposed online, but the article says it is unknown how many have been secured against CVE-2026-46817 attacks.

ARToken: Inside an EvilTokens affiliate panel targeting Microsoft 365 — Should Read

Cisco Talos uncovered ARToken, a sophisticated EvilTokens-linked phishing-as-a-service panel that targets Microsoft 365 with device-code phishing, token theft, PRT persistence, BEC tooling, and SharePoint exfiltration. The research shows the platform has evolved into a full post-compromise operations suite with advanced anti-analysis protections and Cloudflare Workers-based lure infrastructure.

  • Cisco Talos identified a phishing-as-a-service operator panel branded ARToken that shares infrastructure, API contracts, and operational patterns with the EvilTokens platform.
  • The ARToken panel exposes more than 80 API endpoints for device code phishing, Primary Refresh Token persistence, email access, business email compromise operations, and SharePoint exfiltration through a React-based dashboard.
  • Talos recovered two near-identical phishing messages that spoofed a legitimate Wisconsin contractor and targeted an accounts-payable recipient at a U.S. life-sciences company with an outstanding-invoice lure.
  • The ARToken phishing kit uses a seven-layer anti-analysis system that includes user-agent checks, automation detection, browser fingerprinting, interaction telemetry, timing gates, and mouse-movement pattern analysis.
  • The ARToken operator toolkit includes token refresh and sharing, Outlook inbox access, victim email sending, inbox rule creation, SharePoint and OneDrive file operations, Cloudflare Workers deployment management, and a standalone Windows session browser called ARTBrowser.

The SOC Files: ScreenConnect masked as freeware. An inside look at a large-scale campaign — Should Read

Kaspersky uncovered a large SEO-poisoning campaign in which fake freeware sites distribute trojanized installers that silently install ScreenConnect, enabling attackers to deploy AsyncRAT and maintain remote access. The key risk is that legitimate remote administration tools can be abused at scale while appearing trustworthy to users and enterprise defenses.

  • Kaspersky MDR investigated an incident in which ScreenConnect was used to create and run suspicious PowerShell and VBS scripts that ultimately loaded AsyncRAT into a hollowed RegAsm.exe process.
  • The initial ScreenConnect installation was traced to a user-downloaded archive named obs-studio-windows-x64.zip from the typosquatted domain studioobs.com, which mimicked the official OBS Studio site.
  • The malicious archives contained a legitimate Microsoft-signed install.exe file, a rogue install.res.1033.dll library loaded through DLL sideloading, and an Assets folder containing both the impersonated software and ScreenConnect.
  • Kaspersky identified more than 90 spoofed domains in 10 languages that posed as download sites for popular software such as OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, Bandicam, Glary Utilities, and Process Hacker.
  • The malware established persistence with a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater and connected to the AsyncRAT command-and-control domain mora1987.work.gd.

US lifts export controls on Anthropic’s frontier cybersecurity AI models — Should Read

The U.S. lifted export controls that had forced Anthropic to shut down global access to its Fable 5 cybersecurity AI model, after Anthropic agreed to new safeguards, government testing access, and jailbreak reporting measures. The reversal may shape how frontier cybersecurity AI models are regulated going forward.

  • Anthropic restored global access to its Fable 5 model after the U.S. government lifted export controls that had barred foreign nationals from accessing the cybersecurity-focused AI tool.
  • Export controls on Anthropic’s more powerful Mythos 5 model were also fully lifted as of June 30, but access remains restricted to vetted U.S. organizations through Project Glasswing.
  • The initial shutdown was triggered by a technique described in an Amazon research report, but Katie Moussouris argued that the activity was defensive patch testing rather than a guardrail bypass.
  • Anthropic said it trained a new safety classifier that blocks the specific technique in more than 99% of cases, and Commerce Department researchers tested and endorsed the updated safeguards.
  • Anthropic also committed to expanded pre-release government evaluation, rapid disclosure of significant jailbreaks, joint research resources, a shared voluntary security standard, and a HackerOne bug bounty program for cyber jailbreak submissions.

Browser-Only Ransomware: From LLM Hallucinations to a Practical Attack Technique — Should Read

Check Point researchers show that LLM-generated malware ideas can turn theoretical browser risks into practical attacks, including a browser-only ransomware technique that abuses Chrome’s File System Access API after a user grants folder access. The most concerning scenario is Android, where fake AI photo tools could persuade users to allow access to photo directories, enabling file theft or encryption without installing an app or exploiting a vulnerability.

  • Check Point Research analyzed nearly 3,000 files attributed to DeepSeek and found 1,383 files classified as malicious or dangerous by VirusTotal detection or static source analysis.
  • A DeepSeek-attributed Python Flask sample called InfernoGrabber v9.0 included an incomplete browser-based attack scaffold that used the File System Access API to access local files through a victim-facing Discord avatar AI upscaler lure.
  • The browser-native ransomware technique relies on social engineering and a legitimate browser permission prompt, rather than a native payload, APK installation, browser exploit, or root access.
  • Check Point Research tested a controlled proof of concept on Android Chrome and confirmed that Chrome allowed selecting photo directories, including the root of the DCIM folder, for file access after user approval.
  • The article states that, at the time of analysis, there was no evidence that this browser-native ransomware technique had been adopted as an in-the-wild malware pattern.

Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029 — Should Read

Microsoft is accelerating its shift to post-quantum cryptography, aiming to make critical products and services quantum-safe by 2029 as the risk of practical quantum attacks appears to be moving closer. The company says organizations should start building cryptographic inventories and crypto-agile systems now to prepare for migration.

  • Microsoft is accelerating its quantum safe security roadmap and aims to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029.
  • Microsoft plans to incorporate post-quantum cryptography requirements into its Secure Future Initiative.
  • Microsoft identified key focus areas including adopting TLS 1.3, building crypto-agility for stored data, and transitioning trust chains such as code signing, certificate issuance, key protection, and update pipelines to post-quantum cryptography.
  • The development follows a U.S. executive order setting deadlines for federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography.
  • Google and Cloudflare have also announced plans to move toward post-quantum cryptography by 2029.

Where Severity Scores Go Wrong: “Just Add Prototype Pollution” — May Read

JFrog argues that many Axios prototype pollution gadget CVEs are overrated because they are only exploitable when a separate prototype pollution flaw already exists in the same JavaScript runtime. The key takeaway is that severity scores should be treated as a starting point and validated against real-world exploit prerequisites and application context.

  • JFrog researchers found that 10 Axios CVEs disclosed since April 2026 require a pre-existing prototype pollution vulnerability in the same JavaScript runtime context to be exploitable.
  • The article’s proof of concept chained Lodash CVE-2019-10744 with Axios CVE-2026-44494 to pollute Object.prototype.proxy and route Axios requests through an attacker-controlled proxy.
  • JFrog states that Axios is not responsible for creating the prototype pollution condition, but acts as a gadget that consumes a polluted property and turns it into a security impact.
  • The demonstrated attack chain can enable traffic interception, response modification, and credential theft when sensitive information is transmitted through Axios.
  • JFrog argues that CVSS and similar severity scores should be treated as a starting point because they may reflect the impact of a complete attack chain rather than the standalone risk introduced by the affected component.

StepSecurity Maintained Actions Are Now Free for Public Repos — May Read

StepSecurity is making its Maintained Actions catalog free for public GitHub repositories, giving open-source projects secure drop-in replacements for risky, abandoned, or compromised third-party GitHub Actions. Private repositories still require a StepSecurity subscription.

  • StepSecurity announced that its Maintained Actions are now free for public GitHub repositories, while private repositories require a StepSecurity subscription.
  • StepSecurity Maintained Actions are secure, drop-in replacements for popular third-party GitHub Actions and are designed to be API-compatible with the originals.
  • The March 2025 compromise of tj-actions/changed-files, used by over 23,000 repositories, exposed CI/CD secrets in workflow logs and was detected by StepSecurity's Harden-Runner.
  • StepSecurity says its maintained actions use controls including manual secure code review, strict access control, branch and tag protection, secure release processes, dependency monitoring, and monthly upstream synchronization.
  • StepSecurity currently offers 500 maintained actions and provides Policy-Driven PRs for enterprise customers to replace supported third-party actions across repositories automatically.

Four Days Old, Fully Authenticated: CEO Coaching International Impersonation Targets a Sports Technology Company — May Read

Attackers used a four-day-old lookalike domain to impersonate CEO Coaching International and send a fake overdue invoice with collections pressure to a sports technology company. Although SPF, DKIM, and DMARC passed through SendGrid, header inspection revealed the authenticated sending domain did not match the claimed sender, exposing the fraud.

  • Attackers registered the lookalike domain ceo-coachinginc.com on March 6, 2026, and used it on March 10, 2026, to send an invoice fraud email to a sports-technology company.
  • The email impersonated CEO Coaching International and claimed to be from a fabricated persona, Carol Smith, CPA, while pressuring the recipient with an overdue invoice and a collections-agency threat.
  • The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, but the DKIM-signing and Return-Path domain was an unrelated SendGrid domain rather than ceo-coachinginc.com.
  • The Reply-To addresses included carol@ceo-coachinginc.com and admin@azur-email.com, giving the attackers two inboxes to receive responses.
  • Themis scored the message at 63% confidence, and IRONSCALES surfaced it for analyst review based on combined signals including the new domain, domain mismatch, financial theme, and urgency language.

2026 Cybersecurity Assessment: The Gap Between Awareness and Resilience — Optional

Bitdefender’s 2026 Cybersecurity Assessment finds that organizations increasingly understand risks like AI misuse, attack surface exposure, and breach transparency, but struggle to turn that awareness into real operational resilience. The biggest gap is not knowing what matters, but having the visibility, resources, and culture to act on it effectively.

  • The 2026 Bitdefender Cybersecurity Assessment surveyed 1,200 IT and cybersecurity professionals across six countries and found a gap between cyber risk awareness and operational resilience.
  • While 51.8% of respondents believe they have full visibility into sanctioned and unsanctioned AI use, 47.4% say they have only partial or no visibility into Shadow AI tools or personal AI accounts used for work.
  • Respondents identified maintaining hardening policies and exceptions, fear of disrupting business operations, and limited resources as the biggest obstacles to reducing the attack surface.
  • Security professionals ranked AI-related threats as their top three concerns, but Bitdefender Labs found that 84% of high-severity attacks leveraged Living off the Land techniques while only one in five respondents ranked those attacks among their top three concerns.
  • More than half of respondents who experienced a breach in the previous twelve months said they were instructed to keep the incident confidential despite believing authorities should have been notified, with the figure rising to 68.6% in the United States.

Secure Registry now tells you which machine pulled a compromised package — Optional

StepSecurity’s Secure Registry now shows exactly which developer machine or CI pipeline pulled a suspicious or compromised npm/PyPI package, helping teams quickly scope incidents. The feature adds source attribution to package request audit logs so responders can identify and investigate affected systems without searching scattered install logs.

  • On June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added easy-day-js, a typosquat of the dayjs date library, as a dependency across more than 140 packages in the Mastra AI framework ecosystem.
  • The malicious easy-day-js version included an obfuscated postinstall dropper that downloaded a second-stage payload from an attacker-controlled server and then deleted itself.
  • StepSecurity’s Secure Registry now adds Source and Source Identifier columns to its audit log to attribute every npm and PyPI request to the machine or pipeline that made it.
  • A request from a developer machine can link to that device’s page, while a request from GitHub Actions can link to the corresponding Harden-Runner workflow run.
  • Secure Registry attribution is opt-in and works by appending an identifier suffix to the registry auth token for developer machines or CI/CD pipelines.

Microsoft fixes GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel — Optional

Microsoft fixed broken GIF search in the Windows 11 Emoji Panel for newer Windows 11 versions by switching from Tenor to GIPHY in the optional KB5095093 update. Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 are still awaiting a fix.

  • Microsoft fixed the GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel for Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, and 26H1 users by switching from Tenor to GIPHY in the preview KB5095093 cumulative update released on June 23.
  • The GIF feature stopped working for some users on June 30 because Google’s Tenor GIF search engine retired its application programming interface on that date.
  • Microsoft said users can restore GIF functionality by installing the latest available Windows updates, including KB5095093.
  • Microsoft is still working on a fix for the same issue on Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 and has not provided a timeline.
  • The KB5095093 preview update also includes the Point-in-Time Restore feature and fixes a Recycle Bin confirmation dialog bug that showed internal file names instead of normal filenames.

Monthly Newsletter – July 2026 — Optional

Health-ISAC’s July 2026 newsletter highlights upcoming European and Americas Summit deadlines and promotions, plus new cybersecurity resources on autonomous AI vulnerability discovery, third-party IAM, human risk management, and healthcare-focused vulnerabilities.

  • Health-ISAC’s July 2026 newsletter highlights the 2026 Health-ISAC European Summit, scheduled for October 12–16, 2026, in Crete, Greece, with early bird registration and subsidized hotel accommodations.
  • The newsletter announces a post-Summit Friday excursion to Knossos Palace with an expert guide and a seaside lunch featuring Cretan cuisine.
  • For the 2026 Health-ISAC Fall Americas Summit in Tucson, Arizona, abstract submissions close on July 10, and registration opens September 9 with a one-day US$99 member sale.
  • Health-ISAC and Quest Diagnostics conducted joint research on Claude Mythos, an Anthropic AI model described as having autonomous zero-day vulnerability discovery and weaponization capabilities.
  • The newsletter points to new resources on managing third-party identity and access management, human risk management, and vulnerabilities aimed at the healthcare industry.

Japanese insurer, brewer, manufacturer and telecom disclose cyber breaches — Optional

Several major Japanese companies, including Aflac Japan, Sapporo subsidiaries, Nidec and KDDI, disclosed separate cyber incidents involving exposed customer data, suspected data theft, ransomware claims and compromised email credentials. The largest was Aflac Japan, where personal information for about 4.38 million policyholders was exposed.

  • Several major Japanese companies disclosed cyber incidents over the past two weeks that exposed customer data, disrupted internal systems, and prompted investigations.
  • Aflac Life Insurance Japan said hackers compromised its customer portal and other systems, exposing personal information for about 4.38 million policyholders, including names, addresses, and phone numbers.
  • Sapporo Holdings disclosed suspected unauthorized access affecting Singapore-based Pokka and Canadian brewer Sleeman Breweries, but said it had found no impact on domestic operations.
  • Nidec disclosed a ransomware breach affecting part of the network at its Taiwanese subsidiary Nidec Chaun Choung Technology, and the BlackField ransomware group later claimed responsibility.
  • KDDI Corporation said attackers exploited a vulnerability in third-party software to gain unauthorized access to an email system it operates for five Japanese internet service providers.

Martin Lee: Running through the Arctic (and the threat landscape) — Optional

Cisco Talos profiles Martin Lee, EMEA Lead, tracing his accidental path from human virus genetics to early internet work and then cybersecurity through spam filtering and early APT detection. His main advice is to stay curious, visible, and open to unexpected career pivots.

  • Martin Lee is the EMEA Lead at Talos, where his work is largely ambassadorial and focused on explaining changes in the threat landscape to customers and partners.
  • Lee began his career researching the genetics of human viruses before leaving academia after discovering the internet early on and deciding it would become a major invention in his lifetime.
  • After the dot-com crash left him with less work than he wanted, Lee took a job writing spam filters before spam filters and spam itself were major problems.
  • While writing spam filters, Lee and his team began detecting unusual attacks that they later understood were among the first APT attacks.
  • The article says Lee’s cybersecurity career began largely by accident and has continued for more than 23 years as the field has grown significantly.

item.title: Tomcat CVE-2026-55957, Adobe ColdFusion RCE Patches, and AI Hallucinated Domain Abuse
Azure CLI Password Sprays, ClickFix Payloads, GitHub Revocation, and Citrix NetScaler CVEs
Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 Access and Rolls Out Claude Sonnet 5
CitrixBleed CVE-2026-8451, BioShocking AI Browser Abuse, and PyPI Telegram Bot Backdoors
Langflow CVE-2026-33017, Malicious AI Extensions, and GuardFall Agent Risks
SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558 Exploitation, ToddyCat Umbrij, and Aflac Japan Breach Lead Digest
